title: Biological Approaches
learningObjectives: Explain how genetics and twin studies contribute to understanding personality
Describe the role of temperament in personality development
Discuss the relationship between brain structures, neurochemistry, and personality traits

keyTerms.definition: Innate, biologically-based tendencies in emotional reactivity and self-regulation that appear early in life and remain relatively stable.
The proportion of variation in a trait within a population that can be attributed to genetic differences among individuals.
A field that examines how genes and the environment contribute to individual differences in behavior and personality.
Research comparing identical and fraternal twins to estimate the relative contributions of genetics and environment to traits.
A neurotransmitter involved in mood regulation, sleep, and appetite that has been linked to personality dimensions such as agreeableness and neuroticism.

contentBlocks.title: Genetics and Personality
contentBlocks.body:
Biological approaches to personality examine the extent to which our traits are rooted in our biology -- our genes, brain structures, and neurochemistry. The field of **behavioral genetics** uses methods such as **twin studies** and adoption studies to estimate the **heritability** of personality traits.

Identical (monozygotic) twins share 100% of their DNA, while fraternal (dizygotic) twins share approximately 50%. If identical twins are more similar on a personality measure than fraternal twins, this suggests a genetic contribution. Research using this method consistently shows that personality traits are moderately heritable, with genetic factors accounting for roughly 40-60% of the variation in traits like extraversion, neuroticism, and conscientiousness.

Adoption studies provide additional evidence. Children who are adopted tend to be more similar in personality to their biological parents than to their adoptive parents, further supporting a genetic contribution. However, heritability does not mean destiny. The remaining 40-60% of variation is attributable to environmental factors, including unique experiences that are not shared with siblings (called **nonshared environment**). Shared family environment, surprisingly, appears to have a relatively small influence on personality in adulthood.

contentBlocks.title: Temperament, Brain, and Neurochemistry
contentBlocks.body:
One of the strongest pieces of evidence for biological influences on personality is **temperament** -- the innate, biologically-based tendencies that are observable from the earliest weeks of life. Researchers Alexander Thomas and Stella Chess identified three broad temperament categories in infants: **easy** (adaptable, positive mood, regular habits), **difficult** (irritable, irregular habits, intense reactions), and **slow-to-warm-up** (cautious, gradually adapting to new situations). These early temperamental patterns often, though not always, predict personality characteristics later in life.

At the neurological level, personality differences have been linked to variations in brain structure and function. Hans Eysenck proposed that introverts have higher baseline levels of cortical arousal than extraverts, leading introverts to seek less stimulation and extraverts to seek more. Contemporary research using brain imaging has supported aspects of this idea, finding that extraverts tend to show greater activation in reward-related brain areas.

Neurochemistry also plays a role. The neurotransmitter **serotonin** has been associated with mood stability and agreeableness, while **dopamine** is linked to reward sensitivity and extraversion. Levels of the stress hormone **cortisol** have been connected to neuroticism and anxiety proneness. However, the relationship between any single neurotransmitter and a complex personality trait is never simple or one-to-one; personality emerges from the interaction of multiple biological systems with environmental influences.

contentBlocks.title: Temperament from Infancy to Adulthood
contentBlocks.body:
Longitudinal research by Jerome Kagan studied children classified as behaviorally inhibited (shy, cautious, easily distressed by novelty) or behaviorally uninhibited (sociable, bold, approaching novel situations eagerly) at four months of age. Kagan found that many children classified as inhibited in infancy continued to show reserved, cautious behavior in childhood and adolescence. However, the relationship was not deterministic: some initially inhibited children became more outgoing over time, especially when their parents gently encouraged them to try new experiences rather than allowing them to completely avoid novelty.

This research illustrates the interplay between biological predispositions and environmental experiences. **Temperament** provides a biological starting point, but the social environment can shape how that temperament is expressed in personality.

Biological approaches demonstrate that personality has significant genetic and neurological underpinnings. Twin and adoption studies reveal moderate heritability for major personality traits. Temperament, observable from infancy, provides an early biological foundation that environmental factors subsequently shape. Brain structures, cortical arousal levels, and neurotransmitter systems all contribute to individual differences in personality, though these biological factors always interact with experience.

freeTextPrompt.prompt: Consider the finding that personality traits are roughly 40-60% heritable based on twin studies. What does this mean, and what does it not mean? Explain why high heritability does not imply that personality is fixed or that the environment does not matter. Use examples from this section to support your explanation.
freeTextPrompt.rubric: A strong response should: (1) correctly define heritability and explain that it refers to variation within a population, not the degree to which an individual's trait is genetic, (2) explain that heritability does not mean immutability, (3) provide examples showing how environment interacts with genetic predispositions (e.g., temperament research, nonshared environment), (4) avoid common misconceptions about heritability, and (5) demonstrate nuanced thinking about the nature-nurture interaction.
